speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor, Lewis Fagen, tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got a job at Steyr-Daimler, I put in my application that I am a motorcycle repairman, which I really was not. And I was accepted. They put me into the motorcycle division, of which the head was a Polish man by the name of Mura, which was extremely, extremely anti-Semitic. And he knew-- when I came, and he asked me questions-- he knew that I am not a real mechanic. Instead of being a little helpful to me, he wanted to destroy me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, here is a motorcycle. You take it apart, clean up the parts, replace the parts that are worn, and put it together. These were all army motorcycles from the fron-- coming from the front. Well, I took it apart, surely enough. I cleaned it up. But I didn't know how to put it together. And I was struggling and struggling. And finally, I put together the motorcycle. He says, oh, you have it put together. All right. Start it. And I kick the starter. And the engine exploded. So he happily ran to the director of the work school-- was a German-- to report that a Jewish mechanic exploded a motorcycle. And then I was called to the director right away. And he said, this is sabotage, and I am calling Gestapo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in those circumstances, I was a dead man already. However, there was a Jewish mechanic there, who was really a fine mechanic, who wanted to save the situation. And he said to the director-- look, he says. There was one mistake that Mura made, he says-- he threw it on the Pole. He gave him the wrong part and this is why that engine exploded. It's not really all his fault. Let him work under me. And he's a good mechanic. And you need good mechanics. Please don't call the Gestapo. And he actually rescued me from-- from that problem. Because otherwise, I was a dead man for sure. Well, I worked there for a while. And life in ghetto-- you wanted to know-- life in ghetto was-- there were-- we had ups and downs. We were surrounded by a wall. And we had few entrances into the ghetto, which were manned by Polish police, which was officered by German policemen. There was a chief of German police, whose name was Kunde, who was a horrible man. But on the other hand, there was another officer of the German police by name of [? Bosco, ?] who not only they were sympathetic-- not only let people out who were wanting to escape from ghetto, nor let people in who wanted to get into the ghetto, who didn't have the papers-- but eventually, he himself ran away, joined the underground, but eventually was caught by the Germans. He was a lieutenant of German police, who was caught by the Germans and executed. From time to time, they came into houses. They made searches. And they beat up people in the ghetto. There was a Jewish police, which was collaborating with the Germans. There was head of the Jewish police by the name of Simcha Spiro, who was a Hassid-- Hassidic Jew before the war. And all of a sudden, he became-- he was a caricature of a man. He was short and he was bow-legged, thin. Before the war, he had long hair, a long beard. He was a glazier by profession. And he took it to his heart, his terrific position. And he got himself very fancy uniforms with epaulettes, with all kind of things on it, which was really like a carnival. And he was more than willing to cooperate with the Germans. So the Jewish police in the ghetto was not exactly something that I would-- I would want the history to know about. However, there were several Jewish policeman who joined in order to help other people. And there were such people. And there was one occasion where I was saved by one of the policemen. Our life revolve-- turned around. We got up early in the morning. We went with the group to work. We came back to the ghetto. It was late already. We had a little meal, whatever food we could scrounge here and there. In the evening, maybe I met with some friends, or I stayed in our dingy room with six people, and I read a book in the corner someplace. It was nothing to write home about. At one point, the Germans announced that anybody who has foreign citizenship or papers to emigrate should register, because there will be an exchange for German prisoners of war. It so happened that we got American visa number from the embassy. And my father felt that maybe this will save us out of Poland and we will be exchanged. So we registered. And there were about maybe 120 people that registered in the ghetto at that time. The ghetto had about 20,000 people. And we were all prepared that we are going to America and there will be an exchange. And there was-- there was some negotiations going on. We didn't know exactly what was going on, but we knew that some negotiations were going on. At one point-- my father had a schoolmate who was a director of a bank before the war-- director of the foreign department of the bank. And the Germans use him during the war for some foreign currency manipulation. So he became sort of collaborator with the Germans. He did not live in the ghetto. He lived outside of the ghetto, protected by the Gestapo and by the Germans. But he used to come from time to time to ghetto. He met my father on the street. My father was afraid of him, because he was a collaborator. However, he called my father by his first name. He says, I want to talk to you. He says, you've put your name down for an exchange. He says, you have an American visa number. He said, yeah. He says, I would suggest that you take off your name. Well, my father came home, and we were still sitting at the table, debating what are we going to do. And finally, we came to conclusion that if he warned my father like that, there is something in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it cost money to take off the names so nobody would-- so they had to rewrite the whole list, in order to take the name. And my father had to pay off somebody in the Jewish Community Center there, which they called a [NON-ENGLISH]. And sure enough, all the other people were taken, supposedly to an assembly place in Warsaw, and nobody survived. Everybody perished. So this was another lucky thing that the fate brought us.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember about ghetto was June 1942. There came an announcement that they're going to change Kennkarte, which was the identification, for another identification called the Blauschein, the 'blue card.' Well, some did receive the Blauschein, and some didn't. My sister, my father, and myself got the Blauschein. My mother didn't. And June, we heard marching, 5 o'clock in the morning. Sure enough, the whole ghetto was surrounded. SS march into the ghetto. They made an announcement that everybody-- every inhabitant of the ghetto-- should report to this-and-this courtyard of a huge building, in which we lived. And it had a huge courtyard. Everybody should report into the courtyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what do we do? My mother doesn't have the Blauschein. So in our room was a little closet. But not this big walk-in closet like we have here, but a little closet. We put my mother into the closet, and we shoved an armoire against the door of the closet, so there was-- the door was not visible. And we all went down to the courtyard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans came. And they were checking everybody's papers. Whoever didn't have the Blauschein they sent outside. And they collected them-- there was a place in Krakow Ghetto called Plac Zgody, which was a square, where most of the deportations were taking part. And they took everybody to this Plac Zgody for deportation-- whoever didn't have a Blauschein. Then, while we are still standing at the square, they made searches from room to room, from house to house. Whoever they found, they either shot on spot-- there were about 1,000 people shot that day in the ghetto-- and-- or they sent them to this Plac Zgody for deportation. We were standing in the courtyard, watching while they walk into our apartment, where my mother was hidden in the closet. And our hearts stopped. And we have seen them bringing people out of other apartments-- little children and women-- dragging them by the hair, and throwing them outside. We heard shots outside, how they were shooting people outside. It was just a horrible thing. But it lasted practically the whole day. When they finally left, and we ran up to the apartment, we pushed away the armoire, we opened the door, and my mother was on the floor, faint. Because there was lack air, and the fright, and everything else. That day, they deported 7,000 people and they killed 1,000. Then the life went on. They brought other people from surroundings of Krakow in place of the 7,000 that they deported. Then came the next action-- what they call an Aktion-- which was in October of 1942, where there were-- everybody had to join his group to go out to work. My father, my mother, and my sister were working in the garment industry. And they let them go out. My group was stopped. And they said that anybody whose group was stopped has to report to the square, Plac Zgody. Well I was left alone. I went home. I put on good boots. I put on, into a knapsack, some warm underwear, and sweaters, and other things. And I said, I'm going to Plac Zgody. While I was walking to Plac Zgody, I met two more friends going to Plac Zgody. So we said, let's keep together, the three of us. As we were-- as we were coming closer to Plac Zgody, there was a Jewish policeman, who belonged to my fraternity before the war. And he was on duty there, on Plac Zgody. And he's seen us coming. And two of us belonged to that fraternity.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took a whip out of his boots. And he started beating us and chasing us away from Plac Zgody. And one of the Germans asked, what's going on? He says, I don't know what they're doing here. They don't belong here. And he chased us into a group that was walking out of ghetto to work. And we walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: During this Aktion, a lot of people were killed on the street. A lot of children were killed. And the people were deported to Belzec, which was a death camp, if you know of Belzec. Actually, if somebody ran away from the train, they got themselves saved. Otherwise, everybody perished that showed up in this Plac Zgody. You must have seen the scenes of Plac Zgody on because this is what they show-- that October Aktion. I mean, this was a horrible thing. But the group that I walked out with was called Wachs. This was a factory manufacturing ventilators for German Navy. So I started working at the Wachs, at the stamping machine. And-- and the same thing happened in the ghetto-- they were coming in. They were searching homes. They were killing people. They were beating up people on the streets. The things were going on daily. I mean, it was-- we got so used to it that it was like a routine thing for us. Well we have seen them coming in. We were afraid to walk on the streets, so we hid. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we hear that at end of '42 that they building a camp for us in Plaszow. And sure enough, they called already some people. And they took them to Plaszow to build a camp. And we didn't know what to expect. By that time, we knew that there were extermination camps already. And we didn't know whether this will be an extermination camp, or labor camp, or whatever. But we were resigned. Whatever will happen will happen. And by that time, around January of 1943, I was the first time that was sent to Plaszow-- was my group, my whole group, the Wachs factory. By March of '43, my mother, my father, and my sister came to Plaszow. It worked that way that we were all barracked, men separately and women separately. And there were three tiers of-- of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they call it [NON-ENGLISH], of bunk beds. And we slept one next to another. And in the middle of the barrack was a little stove that, if somebody wanted to warm up something, cooked for everybody in the barracks. I was going out with my group every single day to Wachs factory. Madritsch, who had the garment factory, established a factory right there in Plaszow, in the camp. And he was employing, at that time, about 800 people. And my father was one of the right hand men of Madritsch. And he got my mother-- and my sister, also-- a job at Madritsch, working at the sewing machines. So he was going every day to work there. And I was going outside of the camp to Wachs factory. This camp was a nightmare. It was not an extermination camp, but any German or Ukrainian guard who work in that camp could shoot anybody on sight that he felt like. Amon Goeth, who was the commander of the camp, was a-- was a beast. He used to ride a big white horse. He had two tremendous dogs. And he was riding the horse all through camp. And if somebody looked at him, and he didn't like his looks, he just took out his pistol and shot. So when he was riding camp, and we were working, or we were walking, we were afraid to look at him. Because if you look at him in such a way that he didn't like it-- he could shoot sometimes 20, 25 people in one day. But he was not the only one. There was untersturmbannfuhrer by name of-- SS, by the name of Yann, who was short and fat. And he was another sadist who was shooting people left and-- he lived there with his wife and a little boy, the 14-year-old son. And their son was riding a horse and shooting people. Then there was another untersturmbannfuhrer by the name-- by name of Muller. And there was-- was another by the name of [? Hujack. ?] This [? Hujack ?] was an absolute killer. He could kill people on sight. Beside that, they used to make Aktions. They used to walk into barracks and beat up people just without a reason, just for the fun of it. The latrines were usually outside of the barracks. If one had a need at night-- let's say in December at 10 degrees-- you had to run to the latrine. You had to run, sometimes, through two, three barracks before you came to a latrine. We already wore stripe suits in that camp. Some-- some of the people had their heads shaven. There were hangings going on. For instance, a good friend of mine escaped and was caught in the city. They brought him back. They hanged seven young boys. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They used a Jewish policeman to hang. And the Jewish policeman was not very willing to do it. But they told him, if you're not going to do it, we hang you. So they used him. And everybody in camp had to come and watch. When my group-- Amon Goeth decided that my group is not going to go out of ghetto anymore. We cannot go out to work. We have to stay in the ghetto. The chief of ghetto police was a man by the name of Chilowicz, who was cooperating with the commander of the Plaszow camp. And Chilowicz picked three young men from my group to become policemen. And I was the lucky one among the three that he picked. Well, in those days, you couldn't say no. So my duty was to stand on the-- on guard. There was a Polish camp within that Jewish camp. And I had to stand at night, on guard, watching the Polish camp. And I would stand the whole night on the tower. But one day, they made an Aktion to get the old people for deportation. And each policeman got 15, 20 names to bring for deportation. And when I got a list of 15, 20 men, I knew three or four people on that list. And I say, I'm not going to bring those people in. So I hid. In my barrack, I hid on the upper tier of the bunk beds. I just lied down. And I was laying there throughout the day. And nobody realized that I didn't bring anybody. But right there and then, I decided I don't want to be a policeman. I don't want to do this-- this kind of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next morning, I went to Chilowicz and I said, look, I don't want to be a policeman. He says, you don't want to be a policeman? I said, no. 'OK, give me your hat.' You know, policemen wore a hat, like an army hat. I gave him the hat. He turns to his secretary. He had a-- another policeman was his secretary. He says, you put him into the quarries as of tomorrow. I said, please, don't. I will be a policeman, but don't-- don't do that to me. He says, no, you cannot be a policeman anymore. You resigned, he says. You're going to the quarries tomorrow. Well, I knew that one cannot last in the quarries more than four or five days. Because the foreman of the quarries-- his name was Ivan-- who was Ukrainian or Russian-- was killing people there. First of all, they got killed by the kind of work they did. And then he was beating them so severely that he was killing them. So there was another officer of the police who knew my father and said to Chilowicz, leave him alone. Why do you do that to him? Now, don't butt in, he says. He's going to the quarries.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I started inquiring from-- of people who were in the quarries what to do. And they said, if you can acquire, somehow, a bottle of some kind of alcohol and bring it to him, you will have peace and quiet for a day, at least. So I did acquire a bottle. I brought it the next morning. I came to the quarries. And he said, OK, you're going to count all the wagons that they push up the mountain with the stones.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I counted the wagons. The next day, I had to bring another bottle, which I did. And I was counting that. I didn't have-- I couldn't acquire another bottle for the third day. So I came-- oh, you don't have a bottle, I see. I says, no. Now, you push the wagons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was pushing the wagons, and I saw-- and he was going with a whip behind us. And I saw that a few more days, this is not for me. So we had to not only push the wagons, but we had to sort of tracks while we are doing it. And they were extremely heavy wagons. And like four people were pushing, one after another. And this was kind of work you know, that was really not needed. This was just in order to persecute us.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my father went to Madritsch and explained the situation to him. And Madritsch, unbeknown to me, went to Amon Goeth-- whom he knew-- and he said, look. He says, I have a problem. I have a lot of sewing machines that were not-- that are idle. And I don't have repair people. And one of my-- supposedly one of the best repair-- sewing machine repairmen is working in the quarries. Can you get him out and get him to me? Otherwise, I cannot finish the garments.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the fourth day, which would be probably the last day that I could last in the quarries-- the quarries were what they called penal-- they called it in German [GERMAN], which was penal-- penal work. On the fourth day, middle of the day, an SS man comes in, and ask-- I hear him asking for my name. And this Ivan calls me. And I say-- he says, come with me. And I didn't know what he was going to do with me. And they brought me up to this Madritsch works. And Madritsch explained to me what he did. And he says, now I want you to repair the sewing machines. So they put me on the night shift into a room with 50, 60 sewing machines. And I didn't know what to do. So I started taking parts from one and putting it in another. And I just tried to do something. And sure enough, I activated three sewing machines the first night, just by exchanging parts. And after a while, I got a whiff of it. And-- and it wasn't such a big deal. And I learned how to fix those sewing machines. In Plaszow, we had to report every morning to what they called Appellplatz, which was a huge field. Each group was standing in rows, like soldiers, and counted. And as they were counted, they let them out to go to work. There were people working, let's say, in shoemaker department. There were people who work in garment industry. There were people who work in metalworks. There were people who work in the paper manufacturing works. People work in all kind of jobs. And each group was leaving from that Appellplatz. One day, they-- maybe I-- one day, they called us to come to the Appellplatz one hour ahead. And we didn't know why. And we all reported one hour ahead on the Appellplatz. And we saw that they were setting up-- the Appellplatz was in sort of a valley. And they were setting up machine guns all around us with SS guards. Now we figured that this is the end. They're going to shoot everybody in camp. And we were standing not-- unbeknown to us what's going to happen. And we stood like this for two, three hours-- four hours maybe. All of a sudden, through loudspeakers, they started playing music. And while they were playing music, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] trucks started coming in on the road facing the Appellplatz. They collected all the little children in the whole camp. And they put them on trucks. And they were taking them away. And that moment, there was like a uprising. Everybody started yelling. And-- and-- and mothers started running. And they were shooting over our heads. And the music was playing. And it came over the loudspeaker, 'Everybody lie down.' And everybody lie down. But some of the mothers wouldn't. They started running toward the trucks. And they were shot. And they took away about 300, 400 children. And they took them to Auschwitz. This was the end.
